title: How to safeguard the project from Ai ?
author: IndependenceWide3583
selftext:
Hello everyone, recently I came across some news and articles stating claude deleting the production codes and some users confirmed all of their work got deleted. 


So how to protect ourselves from that. I don't paste my successfull backtest results to ai so that it doesn't know which one worked.. 

Although I use it to give me the complete code..i'm trying to level up to leverage its power of code but also protecting my IP. 

Please help.

comments.author: Automatic-Essay2175
comments.body:
Listen to me carefully because we don’t have much time. This information is extremely sensitive, and they may be monitoring this thread (you just HAD to mention Claude by name). 

Screenshot this comment, print it out, and then unplug your computer.

Okay ready here is the message:

No one cares about your vibecode slop.

comments.author: Jazzlike_Syllabub_91
comments.body: Use git? Use backups? Any number of strategies organizations use to safeguard their intellectual assets…

comments.author: QuirkyChipmunk1414
comments.body:
Never rely on AI tools to store or manage your actual strategy. Keep the core logic in local repos (Git, backups) and only use AI for small pieces like debugging or ideas.

I also avoid pasting full strategies or parameters.

Sometimes I just use tools like aiphamind AI to glance at macro signals or sentiment, but the real models always stay local.

comments.author: dogazine4570
comments.body:
A few practical safeguards you can put in place:

1. **Never treat an LLM as your source of truth.** Your codebase should live in a proper version control system (Git + remote like GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket). Push frequently. If something gets deleted locally (by you or anything else), you can roll back instantly.

2. **Separate environments.** Don’t give any AI tool direct access to your production repo or live infra. Use a sanitized copy or isolated snippets. If you’re using an IDE plugin, double‑check what folders it can access.

3. **Local + offsite backups.** Automated daily backups to an encrypted external drive or cloud storage. The 3‑2‑1 rule (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite) is boring but effective.

4. **Principle of least privilege.** Don’t run tools with write permissions to critical directories unless absolutely necessary. Read-only where possible.

5. **IP protection mindset.** If your edge is in strategy logic, consider modularizing: let AI help with scaffolding, utilities, tests, etc., but keep core logic abstracted or partially stubbed when prompting.

LLMs don’t autonomously delete production code unless integrated with systems that allow it. The real risk is workflow design, not the model itself. Treat AI like an untrusted junior dev: helpful, but sandboxed and supervised.

comments.author: bjxxjj
comments.body:
First, take those “AI deleted my prod code” stories with a grain of salt. In almost every case I’ve seen, it was either a local file handling mistake, an over‑permissive tool integration, or confusion about what the model can/can’t access. LLMs don’t have autonomous access to your filesystem unless you explicitly wire that up.

That said, you *should* treat AI like any third‑party tool:

1. **Never rely on it as your only copy.** Use proper version control (Git + remote like GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket). Commit early, push often. That alone eliminates 99% of “it got deleted” disasters.

2. **Keep prod isolated.** Don’t run AI agents with write access to your production environment. If you’re using tools with file/system permissions, sandbox them (Docker, separate VM, non‑prod branch).

3. **Limit what you share.** If IP is sensitive, abstract it. Share structure and interfaces, not proprietary logic or datasets. You can stub strategies or replace core logic with placeholders when asking for help.

4. **Backups > paranoia.** Automated backups + versioning beat trying to “hide” successful results from the model.

AI should be a coding assistant, not a source of truth or a deployment pipeline. Keep ownership in your tooling, and you’ll be fine.
